 {"id":"git_safety_guard-4yij","title":"[Explain] Add explanation field to DestructivePattern","description":"# Task: Add explanation field to DestructivePattern

## Parent Epic: Visual Explanations (git_safety_guard-r97e)

## Objective

Extend the DestructivePattern struct to include an optional explanation field that provides human-readable context about why the pattern exists and what could go wrong.

## Current DestructivePattern Structure

Located in src/packs/mod.rs, includes:
- name: String
- description: String  
- pattern/regex: compiled regex
- severity: Severity enum

## Proposed Addition

pub struct DestructivePattern {
    // ... existing fields ...
    
    /// Detailed explanation of why this pattern is dangerous.
    /// Should explain consequences and suggest alternatives.
    pub explanation: Option<String>,
    
    /// Suggested safe alternatives or preview commands.
    pub suggestions: Option<Vec<Suggestion>>,
}

pub struct Suggestion {
    pub label: String,      // e.g., "Preview first"
    pub command: String,    // e.g., "git diff"
    pub description: String // e.g., "See what would be lost"
}

## Implementation Steps

1. Add explanation field to DestructivePattern in src/packs/mod.rs
2. Add Suggestion struct for structured alternatives
3. Update pattern construction macros if any
4. Ensure backward compatibility (field is Option)
5. Update any serialization (JSON output in explain)

## Backward Compatibility

Using Option<String> ensures existing patterns without explanations continue to work. Explanations are additive content.

## Acceptance Criteria

- DestructivePattern has explanation field
- Field is optional and defaults to None
- Existing patterns compile without changes
- JSON serialization includes explanation when present

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-53q","title":"E2E: explain/simulate/scan CLI flows","description":"## Objective

Add E2E coverage for non-hook CLI flows to ensure parity and stability.

## Scope

- `dcg explain` outputs (pretty/json/compact) are structurally valid.
- `dcg simulate` input formats are parsed end-to-end with realistic fixtures.
- `dcg scan` modes (`--staged`, explicit paths, JSON output) behave with expected exit codes.

## Logging

- On failure, dump command output + a minimal context snippet.

## Acceptance criteria

- E2E runs in CI without external dependencies.
- Format outputs are validated with jq or schema checks.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-5apz","title":"Auto-Suggested Allowlists from History","description":"# Epic: Auto-Suggested Allowlists from History

## Priority Score: 700/1000 (Third Priority)

## Background and Rationale

Manually writing allowlist patterns is tedious and error-prone. Users either give up and disable DCG, write overly broad patterns, or spend hours crafting precise patterns.

This feature analyzes the history database to identify commands that are repeatedly blocked but appear safe, then generates suggested allowlist entries with confidence scoring for user approval.

## Example Workflow

User runs: dcg suggest-allowlist

Output shows:
  HIGH CONFIDENCE (blocked 127 times, consistent pattern):
    Commands: npm run build, npm run test, npm run lint
    Suggested pattern: npm run (build|test|lint)
    Risk assessment: LOW (read-only npm scripts)
    Accept? [y/N]

## Technical Approach

1. Query history for denied commands grouped by pattern_triggered
2. Cluster similar commands using normalized edit distance
3. Generate regex patterns that match each cluster
4. Score confidence based on frequency, consistency, risk factors
5. Present interactive CLI for approval/rejection
6. On accept, append to user allowlist config

## Clustering Algorithm

- Normalize all commands
- Tokenize into (command, subcommand, flags, args)
- Group by (command, subcommand, flags)
- For each group, find common argument patterns
- Generate regex from pattern

## Risk Scoring Factors

- Command frequency (more = higher confidence)
- Argument consistency (similar args = higher confidence)
- Dangerous flags present (-f, --force = lower confidence)
- Path sensitivity (system paths = lower confidence)

## Key Files

- src/history/schema.rs - Ensure rule_id tracking
- New: src/suggest.rs - Clustering and pattern generation
- src/cli.rs - Add dcg suggest-allowlist command
- src/allowlist.rs - Config writing integration

## Success Criteria

- Suggestions generated from at least 10 denied commands
- Confidence tiers: HIGH, MEDIUM, LOW with clear criteria
- Interactive approval with risk explanation
- Generated patterns are valid regex
- Config file updated atomically

## Dependencies

Requires rule_id tracking in history (see Rule Metrics epic)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-16T00:04:34.569738697-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-16T00:04:34.569738697-05:00"}
-{"id":"git_safety_guard-5apz.1","title":"[Suggest] Risk scoring + explicit manual confirmation","description":"# Task: Risk scoring + explicit manual confirmation

## Purpose
Suggestions must never auto-write allowlist entries. Each suggestion should include
an explicit risk score and require manual confirmation before writing.

## Requirements
- Risk scoring independent from confidence scoring
- Risk categories: low / medium / high (deterministic rules)
- Interactive CLI must require explicit confirmation per entry
- Non-interactive mode outputs suggestions only (no writes)

## Implementation
- Add `risk_score` to suggestion model
- Define scoring rules (flags like `--force`, destructive verbs, system paths)
- Enforce confirmation in CLI flow

## Acceptance Criteria
- No allowlist entry is written without a user confirmation step
- Risk score is visible in both text and JSON outputs

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-5apz.2","title":"[Suggest] Audit logging for suggested allowlist entries","description":"# Task: Audit logging for suggested allowlist entries

## Purpose
Ensure every accepted suggestion is recorded for traceability and review.

## Requirements
- Log accepted suggestion to history with: rule_id (if any), pattern, risk score,
  user action (accepted/modified), and timestamp
- Include source cluster stats (frequency, sample commands)

## Implementation
- Extend history schema for suggestion events or reuse existing logging channel
- Update suggest-allowlist CLI to emit audit entries on accept

## Acceptance Criteria
- Accepting a suggestion writes an audit record
- Non-interactive mode produces **no writes**

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-5apz.3","title":"[Suggest] Unit tests for clustering + pattern + risk scoring","description":"# Task: Unit tests for clustering + pattern + risk scoring

## Purpose
Ensure core suggestion algorithms behave deterministically and safely.

## Requirements
- Unit tests for clustering (grouping correctness)
- Unit tests for pattern generation (anchoring, no overbroad matches)
- Unit tests for risk scoring rules

## Acceptance Criteria

- Output is deterministic across runs

### Stripe CLI
- `stripe webhook_endpoints delete`
- `stripe customers delete`
- `stripe products delete`
- `stripe prices delete`
- `stripe coupons delete`
- `stripe api_keys roll` (key rotation without coordination)

### API Operations

## Safe Patterns
- `stripe listen` (webhook testing)
- `stripe customers list`
- `stripe products list`
- `stripe payments list`
- `stripe logs tail`
- API GET requests

## Implementation Notes
- Parent category: payment.* (git_safety_guard-atw)
- Keywords: stripe
- Note: Webhook deletion breaks payment notifications

- [ ] Customer deletion blocked
- [ ] Product deletion blocked
- [ ] Key rotation flagged

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-woe","title":"Feature: Memory Leak Detection Tests","description":"# Feature: Memory Leak Detection Tests

## Background

DCG runs as a hook on EVERY Bash command executed by Claude Code. In a typical coding session:
- Hundreds to thousands of commands are executed
- Each command invokes DCG
- Memory leaks accumulate across invocations
- Long sessions could exhaust system memory

CASS has dedicated memory leak tests that run in CI to catch leaks early. DCG needs the same.

## Why This Matters for DCG Specifically

### Usage Pattern
```
User starts Claude Code session
  → Claude executes: git status        → DCG invoked
  → Claude executes: ls -la            → DCG invoked
  → Claude executes: cargo build       → DCG invoked
  → ... (repeat 500+ times)            → DCG invoked each time
Session ends after hours
```

Even small leaks (a few KB per invocation) compound:
- 1000 invocations × 1KB leak = 1MB leaked
- Long session with 5000 invocations = 5MB leaked
- Multiple concurrent sessions = problem

### Critical Code Paths

These DCG components process every command and must not leak:

1. **Input parsing** (`hook.rs`)
   - JSON parsing with serde_json
   - String allocation for command extraction

2. **Pattern matching** (`evaluator.rs`)
   - Regex compilation (lazy_static, but still)
   - Match result allocation

3. **Heredoc processing** (`heredoc.rs`)
   - Content extraction
   - Script parsing
   - AST construction

4. **Output generation** (`trace.rs`, `hook.rs`)
   - JSON serialization
   - Colorful warning formatting

## Technical Approach

### Test Infrastructure

Create `tests/memory_tests.rs` with:

```rust
//! Memory leak detection tests
//! 
//! These tests process many inputs and check that memory usage
//! doesn't grow unboundedly. They must run with --test-threads=1
//! to get accurate measurements.

use std::alloc::{GlobalAlloc, Layout, System};
use std::sync::atomic::{AtomicUsize, Ordering};

/// Custom allocator that tracks allocations
struct TrackingAllocator;

static ALLOCATED: AtomicUsize = AtomicUsize::new(0);

unsafe impl GlobalAlloc for TrackingAllocator {
    unsafe fn alloc(&self, layout: Layout) -> *mut u8 {
        ALLOCATED.fetch_add(layout.size(), Ordering::SeqCst);
        System.alloc(layout)
    }
    
    unsafe fn dealloc(&self, ptr: *mut u8, layout: Layout) {
        ALLOCATED.fetch_sub(layout.size(), Ordering::SeqCst);
        System.dealloc(ptr, layout)
    }
}

fn memory_usage() -> usize {
    ALLOCATED.load(Ordering::SeqCst)
}
```

### Test Cases

1. **Hook input parsing** - Parse 1000 JSON inputs, check memory stable
2. **Pattern evaluation** - Evaluate 1000 commands, check memory stable
3. **Heredoc extraction** - Process 1000 heredocs, check memory stable
4. **Full pipeline** - End-to-end 1000 times, check memory stable

### Memory Budget

Tests should assert:
- Memory growth < 1MB over 1000 iterations
- No single iteration leaks > 10KB
- Final memory within 10% of baseline after GC

## Implementation Tasks

1. **Create memory test infrastructure** - Tracking allocator, helpers
2. **Add hook parsing memory test** - JSON input processing
3. **Add pattern matching memory test** - Evaluator path
4. **Add heredoc processing memory test** - Script extraction
5. **Add memory tests to CI workflow** - Dedicated job

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Memory tests exist and pass locally
- [ ] Memory tests run in CI (dedicated job)
- [ ] Tests catch intentionally introduced leaks
- [ ] Memory growth < 1MB over 1000 iterations
- [ ] Tests run with --test-threads=1 for accuracy

## Considerations

### Test Isolation
Memory tests must run sequentially (--test-threads=1) to get accurate measurements. Other tests running concurrently would pollute measurements.

### CI Resources
Memory tests should run on a dedicated CI job to:
- Avoid interference with other tests
- Have predictable resource allocation
- Allow longer timeouts if needed

### Platform Differences
Memory tracking may behave differently on Linux vs macOS. Focus on Linux (CI environment) but tests should pass everywhere.

### False Positives
Some "leaks" are actually intentional caches:
- Lazy static regex patterns (compile once, keep forever)
- Pack pattern registry (loaded once at startup)

Tests should account for expected one-time allocations.

## References

- CASS memory tests: /data/projects/coding_agent_session_search/.github/workflows/bench.yml (memory-tests job)
- Rust tracking allocator: https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/alloc/trait.GlobalAlloc.html","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:21:21.710163301-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-11T02:48:31.321864332-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-11T02:48:31.321864332-05:00","close_reason":"All dependencies completed: memory test infrastructure (dj8), memory tests (39k), and CI job (8zo) are all closed; tests/memory_tests.rs exists and memory-tests job in CI workflow","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-woe","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-dj8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:25:12.375811198-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-woe","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-39k","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:25:12.406752934-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-woe","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-8zo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:25:12.43467007-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-wso","title":"[CATEGORY] cdn.* - CDN & Edge Pack Suite","description":"# CDN & Edge Pack Suite

## Overview
CDN and edge services cache content globally. Cache purges and config changes affect all users. Origin configuration changes can cause global outages.

## Packs to Implement

### cdn.cloudflare
- **Why Critical**: Cloudflare provides CDN, security, and edge compute. Rule deletion or purge affects global traffic.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**:
  - `wrangler delete` - Deletes Workers
  - `wrangler kv:key delete` / `kv:namespace delete`
  - Cache purge operations
  - Firewall rule deletion
  - Page rule deletion
- **Safe Patterns**: `wrangler whoami`, `list`, `get`

### cdn.fastly
- **Why Critical**: Fastly powers high-traffic sites. Service version activation can cause outages.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**:
  - `fastly service delete`
  - `fastly domain delete`
  - `fastly backend delete`
  - VCL changes via API
- **Safe Patterns**: `describe`, `list`, `version list`

### cdn.cloudfront
- **Why Critical**: AWS CloudFront is deeply integrated with AWS services.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**:
  - `aws cloudfront delete-distribution`
  - `aws cloudfront create-invalidation` (cache purge - costly)
  - Origin configuration changes
- **Safe Patterns**: `list-`, `get-`, `describe-`

## Dependencies
- Depends on: Tier 3 Epic (git_safety_guard-9ic)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Major CDN providers covered
- [ ] Cache purge operations flagged
- [ ] Edge compute deletion detected
- [ ] Configuration changes handled","notes":"Testing/Validation:
- Every cdn.* pack must comply with Standard Pack Task Requirements (git_safety_guard-ikf2), including unit tests and e2e corpus scenarios.
- Category-level e2e summary: ensure at least one allow + one deny scenario per pack appears in shared harness logs.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:29:33.364405237-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-11T03:20:20.960218997-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-11T03:20:20.960218997-05:00","close_reason":"All 3 CDN packs implemented: cdn.cloudflare_workers (12+8), cdn.fastly, cdn.cloudfront (12+7). Category complete.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-wso","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-nys","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:35:20.669026576-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-wso","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-1y6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:13:02.041971709-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-wso","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-foj","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:13:02.075904939-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-wso","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ikf2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:48.924802583-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-wso","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-i79","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:48.97240583-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-wso","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ct5s","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:49.020007723-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-wso","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-jdb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:49.065706797-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-wso","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-e7j","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:49.11446719-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-wso","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-l968","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:49.159146866-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
+{"id":"git_safety_guard-wy6s","title":"[Packs] Integrate ExternalPackLoader into evaluation path","description":"The ExternalPackLoader infrastructure exists in src/packs/external.rs but is NOT integrated into the main evaluation path. Custom packs specified via custom_paths in config are never loaded or evaluated.

## Evidence
- ExternalPackLoader exists with from_config() and load_all() methods
- custom_paths field exists in PacksConfig (src/config.rs:839)
- BUT: main.rs and evaluator.rs do not call ExternalPackLoader
- E2E tests that try to use custom packs via config fail (currently marked #[ignore])

## Files to modify
- src/main.rs: Load external packs and add to evaluation
- src/evaluator.rs: Support evaluating commands against external packs

## Acceptance criteria
- Custom packs from custom_paths are loaded at startup
- External pack patterns are evaluated alongside built-in packs

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-wy72","title":"[PACK] storage.gcs - Google Cloud Storage Pack","description":"# Google Cloud Storage Pack

## Scope
Implement pattern matching for GCS operations.

## Destructive Patterns
### gsutil
- `gsutil rb` - Remove bucket
- `gsutil rb -f` - Force remove bucket
- `gsutil -m rm` - Multi-threaded remove
- Recursive delete operations

### gcloud storage
- `gcloud storage buckets delete`
- `gcloud storage objects delete`
- `gcloud storage rm`

## Safe Patterns
- `gsutil ls`
- `gsutil cat`
- `gsutil stat`
- `gsutil du`
- `gsutil cp` (copy/download)
- `gcloud storage buckets list`
- `gcloud storage objects list`

## Implementation Notes
- Parent category: storage.* (git_safety_guard-skt)
- Keywords: gsutil, gcloud
- Note: Both gsutil and gcloud storage commands

## Testing Requirements
- [ ] Unit tests for bucket removal
- [ ] Unit tests for object deletion
- [ ] Unit tests for recursive operations
- [ ] E2E test: `scripts/e2e_tests/storage_gcs.txt`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Bucket removal blocked
- [ ] Object deletion blocked
- [ ] List/copy operations allowed","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:12:26.208275911-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-11T03:32:36.742771804-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-11T03:32:36.742771804-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented storage.gcs pack with 15 safe patterns and 6 destructive patterns.  {"id":"git_safety_guard-x0va","title":"[Span] Ensure span capture across match sources + original mapping","description":"# Task: Ensure span capture across match sources + original mapping

## Parent Epic: Span Highlighting (git_safety_guard-jpfm)

## Objective
Ensure every deny/warn match path captures a byte span **mapped to the original command**.
This includes pack patterns, legacy patterns, and heredoc/AST matches.

## Why
Highlighting is only trustworthy if the span points to the exact substring the user sees.
Normalization/sanitization can shift offsets; we must either map spans back or recompute
match spans against the original command for display only.

## Implementation Steps
1. Audit evaluator match construction for:
   - Pack patterns
   - Legacy patterns (if any paths still exist)
   - Heredoc/AST matches
2. For pack/legacy patterns: capture span from regex `find()`.
3. For heredoc/AST: map extracted content offsets back to the original command
   (use extraction metadata or re-run a search against original when safe).
4. When mapping is impossible, store a structured fallback reason and rely on
   `matched_text_preview` for display.
5. Add tests that compare expected spans against original command slices.

## Files
- `src/evaluator.rs`
- `src/heredoc.rs`
- `src/ast_matcher.rs`
- `src/trace.rs` (optional: include mapping/fallback notes)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Every deny/warn result has a usable `matched_span` in original command coordinates,
  or a documented fallback reason when mapping cannot be proven.
- No behavior change in allow/deny decisions.
- No performance regression on hot path.

## Notes
This task focuses on **span correctness**, not formatting. Formatting is handled
by the highlight formatter task.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-x3g","title":"[CATEGORY] dns.* - DNS Management Pack Suite","description":"# DNS Management Pack Suite

## Overview
DNS is critical infrastructure. Incorrect DNS changes can make services unreachable. Zone deletion or record manipulation affects all users globally.

## Packs to Implement

### dns.cloudflare
- **Why Critical**: Cloudflare manages DNS for millions of domains. Zone/record deletion causes immediate outages.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**:
  - `cloudflare dns delete` (wrangler)
  - `curl -X DELETE api.cloudflare.com/.../dns_records/`
  - `terraform destroy` on cloudflare resources
- **Safe Patterns**: `list`, `show`, GET requests

### dns.route53
- **Why Critical**: AWS Route53 handles DNS for AWS-hosted services. Hosted zone deletion affects all records.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**:
  - `aws route53 delete-hosted-zone`
  - `aws route53 change-resource-record-sets` with DELETE action
  - `aws route53 delete-health-check`
- **Safe Patterns**: `list-`, `get-`, `test-dns-answer`

### dns.generic
- **Why Critical**: Generic DNS tools (dig, host, nslookup, nsupdate) - nsupdate can modify records.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**:
  - `nsupdate` with delete commands
  - Zone transfers to untrusted servers
- **Safe Patterns**: `dig`, `host`, `nslookup` (read-only)

## Rationale
DNS changes propagate globally with TTL delays. A bad change can take hours to fix even after correction. Zone delegation changes can effectively transfer domain control.

## Dependencies
- Depends on: Tier 2 Epic (git_safety_guard-hhh)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Major DNS providers covered
- [ ] API-based changes detected
- [ ] Record type changes handled appropriately
- [ ] Tests verify propagation-sensitive operations flagged","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:28:25.946071182-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-10T22:19:37.007084357-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-10T22:19:37.007084357-05:00","close_reason":"Completed","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-x3g","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-jt0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:34:31.413213489-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-x3g","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ef4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:34:31.441389982-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-x3g","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-t2r","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:12:56.23175429-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-x3g","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ikf2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:46.932811111-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-x3g","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-i79","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:46.982993279-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-x3g","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ct5s","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:47.030792685-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-x3g","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-jdb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:47.077266947-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-x3g","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-e7j","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:47.122525902-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-x3g","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-l968","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:47.168603157-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
